data.title: EWG: German Stocks' Massive Rally Likely To Pause (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Despite all the tariff talk, European stocks are suddenly all the rage. The major overseas bourses have outperformed the S&P 500 to begin 2025, and no spot has garnered more macro attention than what’s going on in Germany. The Xetra DAX closed last week at an all-time high as it appears the European Central Bank will be aggressively cutting rates in the next 12 months while the US Federal Reserve may be on hold.

The DAX has now gained more than 32% from a year ago, which is better than the S&P 500’s 30% total return. It’s important to point out that the major German stock index is a total return index whereas the SPX is price only. Also, there are currency swings at play. The euro has weakened against the US dollar over the past 12 months, but it seems to be holding the $1.02-$1.03 zone.

Today, I’m revisiting the iShares MSCI Germany ETF (NYSEARCA:EWG). I was bullish on the product way back in November of 2022. Since then, the fund has returned 62%, actually beating the S&P 500 by a few percentage points. With EWG now more expensive compared to Q4 of 2022, I’m downgrading EWG to a hold. The chart is also more suspect with major long-term resistance in play.

German DAX Soaring to All-Time Highs, +32.3% YoY

EWG Among the YTD Country ETF Winners

According to the issuer, EWG seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of German equities. It offers investors exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Germany and is used to express a single-country view.

Now ranked in the top 4% in its Asset Class according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, EWG has significant price strength. Surprising to me, the ETF’s assets under management is just $1.0 billion while the annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, as of February 14, 2025. There’s no stated dividend yield, but historically the fund has paid out $0.70-$0.80 annually, so I contend that the true yield is likely around 2% or just a bit more.

Where EWG shines today is with its share-price momentum. Now A+ rated on the ETF Grading system, it has been a remarkable turnaround from just a handful of months ago when the fund was merely trading sideways. It’s a risky portfolio, however, considering historical volatility trends and its concentrated holding mix. Finally, EWG is highly liquid given almost 900,000 shares traded daily and its 30-day median bid/ask spread of three basis points, per iShares.

For a portfolio refresher, the allocation plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high weight to large caps. Unlike much of Europe, the German stock market is home to many more growth stocks than value, so the DAX may have a higher correlation to the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average than the other major European markets.

Where I have a bit of a concern now is with the price-to-earnings ratio which is near 16x. That’s certainly not high on its own, but just 27 months ago it was more than three turns less expensive. Also consider that the ETF’s long-term earnings growth rate is just 7.9%, so the resulting PEG ratio is not far from two. These figures don’t scream “expensive,” but they are also no longer in bargain territory.

EWG: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

In terms of positioning, like the US stock market, Information Technology is the largest sector, but it’s only 22%; the S&P 500 has a more than 30% weight to I.T. You’ll also find heavy exposure to the cyclical-value areas of Industrials and Financials.

Financials, in particular, have performed very well in the past 52 weeks, which has been a boon for EWG. It has also benefitted from having no Energy exposure – the oil & gas space has been hampered by low WTI and Brent oil prices.

EWG: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, the second half of February has been a tough stretch for EWG over the past 10 years, so we could see the product pare some of its year-to-date gains soon.

Beyond that, the March through May period has been bullish when analyzing the past 10 years of performance data.

EWG: Bullish March-May Seasonality

The Technical Take

Leading me to downgrade EWG from a buy to a hold is its chart. Notice in the graph below that the fund is right back at obvious resistance in the $35 to $37 range. Until proven otherwise, the current price north of $36 is a selling opportunity.

Longer-term, if we see a breakout, then massive upside potential is there. A measured move bullish target to the mid-high $50s would absolutely be in play based on the large $20 trading range since way back in the mid-2000s. This is certainly something to watch as the year progresses.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average – it's rising in its slope, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Of course, we’ve seen this before (2007, 2018, 2021). Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart is in technical overbought conditions, but it has been ranging in a bullish zone between 40 and 80 for the last several months.

Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under today’s price, there should be ample support if we see a protracted pullback.

EWG: Shares Right at Major Long-Term Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on EWG. The valuation is fair and cheap compared to the S&P 500, but the earnings growth trend is not impressive. With the ETF at technical resistance, booking gains after a very strong two-plus years is prudent.

data.title: Life, Liberty, And The Pursuit Of Returns: Celestica, My Top President's Day Stock
data.description:
Is the Stock Market Open on President’s Day?

This Monday, the New York Stock Exchange, Nasdaq, and bond markets will be closed for trading in observance of President's Day. But a day off from trading can be a great time to assess how your portfolio is doing so far in 2025.

This President’s Day, the market is giving us plenty of reasons to feel patriotic, and Top Stocks worth exploring ranked by Seeking Alpha’s Quant System. One of my top President’s Day picks and an Alpha Picks stock: Celestica Inc. (NYSE:CLS), the AI-driven electronic manufacturing services company is making waves in the data center space.

Celestica’s Star-Spangled Rally

Celestica Inc. takes its name from the stars, and its rise over the past year and a half has been nothing short of celestial. Formerly a subsidiary of IBM, CLS has since evolved to become a market leader in electronic manufacturing services and a key technological partner for large-scale data centers, otherwise known as hyperscalers and other AI infrastructure providers. A two-time Alpha Pick, CLS continues to be one of our top-performing stocks, consecutively topping earnings expectations in Q4 2024, with revenues of $2.55B and adjusted EPS of $1.11.

First selected as an Alpha Pick on October 16, 2023, at $25.65 per share, CLS is currently trading at $131.02 per share, and has returned over 384% since its addition to the AP portfolio and is up +236% over the last year.

Celestica Inc. 1-year Trading Chart

CLS has 18 manufacturing facilities in North America and Asia where it produces a variety of products under its Advanced Technology Solutions (ATS) and Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segments. CLS specializes in AI infrastructure manufacturing and design, which includes data center technologies like networking switches, servers, and storage. Celestica’s CCS segment has ballooned to account for 67% of total revenues and has grown 42% YoY as supercharged demand for artificial intelligence has prompted tech companies to spend big on massive data centers that can handle the sheer level of computing power necessary to keep up in the AI arms race.

CLS Quant Rating History (as of 2/14/25)

Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings offers tools to simplify investment processes. Quant Ratings aim to provide an objective, unemotional, data-driven evaluation of each stock based on over 100 underlying metrics graded across five factors: Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions. Celestica is Quant Rated Strong Buy, so let’s dive into why.

Celestica Inc. (NYSE: CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $15.1B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 2/14/25): 7 out of 549

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 2/14/25): 1 out of 16

CLS currently is ranked the #7 Technology stock and the #1 Electronic Manufacturing Services stock. Celesticahas continued to top the charts, even in what has been a volatile year thus far for U.S. tech companies. Like many of its technology peers, the Toronto, Canada-based company was dealt a blow by the sudden debut of low-cost Chinese AI model DeepSeek last month, losing more than 32.8% between January 24-27 as the chatbot’s launch wiped nearly $1T in value from the U.S. stock market. But, CLS has already charted a recovery, gaining 16.5% more than its pre-DeepSeek valuation by February 5. Following the initial DeepSeek dip, CLS has been projected by some Wall Street analysts as a potential winner from the whole debacle, bolstered by the inevitable rise in AI spending—and thus, increase in demand for more AI infrastructure—that the unexpected competition from China has already triggered across the global technology sector. The Canadian tech innovator upped its guidance for 2025 at the end of January, now expecting revenue to be $10.7B compared to its previous outlook of $10.4B, as tech companies’ insatiable appetite for AI data center space fuels demand for its specialized brand of connectivity solutions.

SA Factor Grades rate investment characteristics on a sector-relative basis. CLS has scored A+’s in Momentum and EPS revisions, a B+ in Growth, a B- in Valuation, and a C+ in Profitability.

CLS Growth

CLS’s growth metrics speak for themselves: forward EPS diluted growth of 34.2%, a 245.9% difference from the 10% sector median. The company’s forward EPS GAAP growth is 35.6%, a 215.6% from the 11.3% sector median, and forward revenue growth of 16.8% is a 162.9% difference to illustrate consistent growth and forward-looking potential across the board.

CLS Stock’s Valuation

CLS has maintained an attractive valuation despite its riveting rally, with a forward PEG of 0.77—a -58.6% difference from the broader sector—showcasing a compelling discount to its peers in a period of otherwise frothy tech valuations. Its one year price performance of 238.2% is a stunning 3,696.6% difference from the 6.3% sector median. CLS’ valuation underpins its ‘A+’ Momentum Grade while its 'B+' Growth Grade is pushed higher by forward EPS growth of 49% and YoY EPS diluted growth of 88%, a more than 1000% difference from the sector median.

Celestica: Why I’m Betting on the Data Center Boom This President’s Day

CLS posted strong financial results in Q3 ‘24, reporting a 19% YoY increase in revenue to $2.55B, compared to $2.14B in Q4 202 and a 58% YoY increase in adjusted EPS. Celestica is also projecting $10.7B in 2025 revenue and 22% EPS growth. Read more from SA’s latest Earnings Call Insights.

CLS & Peers 1Y Performance vs. S&P 500 (as of 2/14/25)

CLS continues to outpace its biggest rivals, including Flex Ltd (FLEX), Jabil Inc. (JBL) Sanmina Corporation (SANM), and Plexus Corp (PLXS), by more than 4x each as a result of its powerful momentum, with one-year price performance of 246%, a 1,987.3% difference from the 11.8% sector median, but there are still risks to consider.

Risks to Consider

Despite CLS’s stellar momentum, robust growth, and impressive Q4 results, several insider transactions suggest potential headwinds. Celestica is a Canadian-based company, and as tariffs continue to be headline news, particularly 25% tariffs on imports from Canada, which likely had some impact on February 6th, when CLS stock fell by around 7%. The stock’s slide may have also been driven by reports that the company’s CEO Robert Mionis had sold 75,000 shares of CLS stock. Certain Wall Street analysts, including SA’s Danil Cereda, downgraded their assessment on CLS to a Hold out of caution.

Also worth noting is CLS’s C+ Profitability Score, which has been weighed down by factors including a gross profit margin of 10.7%, a -78.86% difference from the 50.1% sector median. CLS also has a trailing levered free cash flow margin (FCF) of 3.25%, a -72.4% difference to the 11.8% sector median. However, an A+- asset turnover ratio of 1.62x shows that the company continues to reinvest heavily in its future operations.

Concluding Summary

If you have time to sit down and explore some potential new investments this long weekend, CLS makes for a compelling growth/value play thanks to its supercharged momentum, fantastic earnings results, and the SA Quant Team’s Strong Buy rating. This President’s Day, enjoy life, liberty, and the pursuit of returns.

Alternatively, if you're seeking a limited number of monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks, consider exploring Alpha Picks. Alpha Picks employs a transparent and systematic strategy that combines the expertise of SA’s Quantitative Team, our unique data, and the computational power of the systems we have built. Let SA’s Quant Team do the research for you in building a market-beating, data-driven portfolio for long-term growth.

data.title: Sun Life Financial: A Drama-Free Growth Story
data.description:
Sun Life Financial: A Buy Despite A Disastrous Q4

When I first started my analysis on Sun Life Financial Inc. (TSE:SLF, NYSE:SLF), its Q4'24 report was due in a few days. Admittedly, I was optimistic about the stock, but the latest report came, and I was tempted to downgrade it to a hold. However, given the reasons for the challenging quarter, I’m convinced that we have an even better buying opportunity instead.

Company Overview

Sun Life Financial Inc. is a financial services company that offers its services in many countries. It built remarkable momentum in Asian Markets like Hong Kong and the Philippines while maintaining a solid market presence in Europe. However, most of its business comes from North America.

Sun Life Financial offers three primary services: Wealth and Asset Management, Group Health and Protection, and Individual Protection Services.

Sun Life Segment Weight By Revenue (FY2024)

Note: All dollar amounts are presented as CAD, and in $millions unless otherwise stated.

Business Segment

Revenue

Percentage

Wealth Sales & Asset Management

196,074

97.17%

Group - Health & Protection

2,737

1.36%

Individual Protection

2,983

1.48%

Total

201,794

100%

Source: Sun Life Financial

Recent Financial Performance

Sun Life Financial's Q4'24 financials present mixed signals, which is the primary reason for my initial “hold” rating. Its reported quarterly net income significantly decreased by 68.36% to $237 million—one of the most concerning declines in the report and reported ROE decreased from 14% to 4% year-over-year.

The full-year net income grew 3.43% to $3.86 billion, thanks mostly to its Q2 and Q3 results. However, the dismal Q4 caused the company to miss its quarterly and full-year estimates.

Sun Life’s Full-Year Revenue Per Region/Segment

Segment/Operations

2024

2023

Change (-/+)

Canada

$15,588

$17,162

(9.17%)

US

$13,101

$13,500

(2.96%)

Asia

$3,531

$4,032

(12.43%)

Asset Management

$6,672

$6,140

8.66%

Corporate

$404

$538

(24.91%)

Source: Sun Life 2024 Annual Report

Sun Life faces both headwinds and pockets of opportunity across its segments. Although most geographical segments show declines, Asset Management's 8.66% growth presents as a silver lining, though it alone can’t yet offset the rest. The more notable declines in Asia (-12.43%) and Corporate (-24.91%) segments. However, what caught my attention is the firm’s home market in Canada – it decreased by 9.17%, which potentially represents near-term challenges that need observation.

U.S. Market

On the other hand, its U.S. business operations experienced a minimal 2.96% decline. Though it’s not a growth driver, the 2.96% decrease isn’t the end of the world, considering it's in among the costliest places for healthcare.

In fact, the reason for the decline in the U.S. was due to “unfavorable morbidity experience in medical stop-loss,” indicating a high volume of claims in the country. Call me an optimist, but unless there’s an ongoing apocalypse, these things don’t drag on for quarters upon quarters, so I think I can safely say this is a temporary setback. Further, when an insurer loses money on claims, they will increase premiums - just as what would happen to your car insurance if you had enough “expensive” and at-fault claims.

Sun Life Financial Inc. Against its Peers

While it’s true that Sun Life is currently experiencing some tough times compared to its previous quarters, let’s see how it measures up against its closest competitors.

5-YR Net Income

Source: Seeking Alpha

As you can see from the chart above, Sun Life Financial’s net income is on a relatively stable trajectory with a slight positive trend over the past 5 years. The company’s net income is also more consistent than its peers, like PFG, which shows a concerning decline to negative territory. Or, MFC, which experienced notable volatility with a pretty sharp dip in 2022.

Sun Life’s notable competitor is Prudential, with a higher net income of over $4 billion. However, Prudential's earnings appear much more volatile than many investors might prefer.

5-YR Dividend History

Sun Life Financial's forward annual dividend of $2.35 reflects a healthy 4.31% yield. Its payout ratio of 47.04% is also on the low side, which means the firm provides sufficient shareholder value while leaving enough room for reinvestment in the business—or, more realistically, as a buffer against headwinds.

The ratio may be relatively low at first glance, but it’s a somewhat different perspective when you consider Sun Life’s peers.

Companies/Peers

Div. Yield (FWD)

Annual Payout (FWD)

Payout Ratio

Sun Life Financial (SLF)

4.31%

$2.35

47.04%

Prudential Financial (PRU)

4.83%

$5.40

40.15%

Principal Financial Group (PFG)

3.45%

$2.92

40.61%

Manulife Financial (MFC)

3.78%

$1.12

42.19%

Unum Group (UNM)

2.23%

$1.68

19.25%

Though Sun Life’s payout ratio is higher on this selection, 47% is still well below the threshold of concern, and it is the second-highest when it comes to yields. Investors will also appreciate that the company has maintained an attractive 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 8.45%.

Is SLF Stock a Buy?

Source: Seeking Alpha

SLF stock trades at around 10.59x forward earnings, and the stock is up about 17% over the past five years. It’s up over 10% in the last year alone (including the recent dip), not including dividends.

Here’s how Sun Life compares P/E-wise to its competitors:

Companies/Peers

P/E GAAP (FWD)

Sun Life Financial (SLF)

10.59

Prudential Financial (PRU)

7.87

Principal Financial Group (PFG)

10.37

Manulife Financial (MFC)

11.24

Unum Group (UNM)

8.23

Peer P/E Ratio Median

10.37

Source: Seeking Alpha

When I first started my analysis on Sun Life, the stock was up 20% in the last five years. It lost roughly 8% after the Q4 announcement. That said, the stock is a bit higher than its peer group, suggesting it’s fairly valued.

Next Potential Catalyst: Asian Markets

Although circumstances were a bit unfavorable, Sun Life’s business in Asia appears well-positioned to be its next potential growth driver for the firm. Its operations in Asia went from over $3 billion in net loss to a $3.35 billion gain from December 2022 to December 2024, which reflected around 217% over the said period. In my book, this is nothing short of remarkable.

However, Sun Life Asia’s net income declined 20.61% from $592 million to $470 million year-over-year, which includes a one-time impairment charge of $186 million in its Vietnam business. Regardless, net income is still up by 123.8% from its 2022 levels of $210 million, and in my view, what’s more important is how they’re positioning themselves for future growth.

The firm doesn’t seem to take the typical Western approach of treating Asia as an afterthought. Sun Life wants to accelerate its momentum in Asia. The firm has established its foothold in eight major Asian markets and serves 30 million clients. This is more than a “growth opportunity” in markets where the middle class is growing like wildfire, and financial services are highly sought-after.

Source: Sun Life Financial

Indeed, the business model is well-accepted for these markets. Sun Life has struck a balance between wealth management (42%), protection products (30%), and group benefits (28%). And, in my experience, this level of diversification is perfect for most emerging economies – you have wealth management for the growing affluent class, basic protection products for first-time buyers, and group benefits for the expanding corporate sector.

But of course, Sun Life has an established presence in 28 markets worldwide, and with $1.54 trillion in assets under management, it has the means to execute its objectives in Asia.

Insurance Sales In Asian Countries (Q4’24)

Let’s have a more granular look at the sales figures across its operations in Asia. The Hong Kong Market takes the lead with over $1.42 billion in insurance sales as of Q4’24, which represents a 92.57% increase from the recorded sales of Q3’24. I think it aligns with the company's diversified strategy, as Hong Kong's financial market demands all three of its services.

The firm's positioning in high-growth potential markets is also noteworthy – India and the Philippines pulling in sales of $319 million and $242 million, respectively.

Asian Countries

Insurance Sales

Hong Kong

$1.425 Billion

India

$319 Million

Philippines

$242 Million

Malaysia

$74 Million

Vietnam

$66 Million

Indonesia

$58 Million

China

$36 Million

Other Asian Countries

$223 Million

Source: Sun Life Financial

And, although moderate as compared to others, Malaysia, China, and Indonesia have substantial room for expansion – all these markets have a rapidly growing economy and increasing financial literacy, which means untapped potential that could drive Sun Life's next phase of growth, and also the primary reason of my hold rating for the SLF stock.

Price Action After The Q4 Report

Earnings season is often a very volatile time in the market, and it seems like everyone’s got their finger on the trigger. But there’s a lot of money to be made during these times. While there are justifiable reasons why investors should sell Sun Life after the latest report, the smart money looks at the future, not the past.

Consider this: on the day the Q4 report was published, SLF was down about 9% from its previous close of $84.63 on Feb 12, 2025.

Remember, the Q4 report was unilaterally taken as bad news. And still, that means some buyers are scooping up shares at a discount. Combined with my assumption that Sun Life’s Q4 issues are temporary, I would rate SLF as a moderate buy opportunity.

Final Verdict on SLF

I've looked at countless financial stocks, and Sun Life Financial remains notable—though it might be because I'm Canadian. Patriotism aside, with Sun Life, you get a steady company that pays reliable dividends and has less relative volatility than its peer group. It’s a company with proven to work through near-term challenges. So, if you're an investor already holding financials, it would make sense to maintain your position while we await more growth signals. If you’re shopping around for financial stocks, consider Sun Life for its discounted price and strong position in the growing Asian markets.

data.title: Wells Fargo: Using A 5.8% Preferred Stock To Bet On Lower Interest Rates
data.description:
Introduction

I don’t think Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) needs a lengthy introduction, as pretty much every investor likely is familiar with this large US financial institution. In this article, I will have a closer look at one specific series of preferred shares trading at a discount of almost 20% to its par value, and this paves the way to generate a capital gain if or when the interest rates on the financial markets decrease.

The bank needs a small fraction of its profits to cover the preferred dividends

While the focus of this article will be on the preferred equity, it only makes sense to have a look at how the bank is performing. After all, I’d like to make sure the preferred dividends enjoy an excellent coverage ratio.

In the final quarter of 2024, Wells Fargo announced a net interest income of $11.8B, which represents a QoQ increase of approximately 1%. That of course is a positive evolution, but unfortunately, the bank’s net non-interest expenses increased from $4.4B to approximately $5.3B with a substantial increase in personnel expenses as the main culprit.

This resulted in a pre-tax and pre loan loss provision income of almost $6.5B and after deducting the $1.1B in loan loss provisions and the $120M tax bill, the net profit was approximately $5.26B of which $5.08B was attributable to Wells Fargo. The income statement above also shows the total cost of the preferred dividends was just $278M, which means the bank needed less than 6% of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends. This indicates the preferred dividends are well covered.

That being said, keep in mind the bank definitely benefited from a nice tax benefit in the final quarter of the year. According to the management team, there was a $863M tax benefit related to resolution of other matters, but there were some other non-recurring items as well. The bank recorded $647M in severance expenses while there was a $448M loss on debt securities. So even if we would take the normalized taxes into account, there were some other non-recurring expenses, and on an adjusted basis (removing all non-recurring items), the adjusted net profit would have been (slightly) higher than in the preceding quarter.

A closer look at the Series Z preferred shares

Wells Fargo has several different issues of preferred shares outstanding, but just like a little while ago when I looked at JPMorgan’s preferred shares, I was looking for a low coupon. After all, the current yield of most preferred shares is the same, but as I still think the interest rates on the financial markets will continue to move down, preferred stock with a low fixed preferred dividend yield may provide the potential to generate capital gains as well.

The Series Z preferred shares (NYSE:WFC.PR.Z) currently offer a fixed preferred dividend of $1.1875 per year for a 4.75% yield on the principal amount of $25/unit.

This series of preferred shares is currently trading at $20.47 which means it currently offers a yield of 5.80%. A sub-6% yield definitely isn’t high, but as mentioned before, I like the potential to generate a capital gain if/when/as interest rates go down. This means I don’t consider the Series Z preferred shares to be an income-only idea, but a "total return" idea.

The five-year US Treasury Yield is currently approximately 4.30%, and if this benchmark interest rate would move down to, for instance, 3.25% and the market requires a 200 bps mark-up for preferred equity issued by Wells Fargo, the Series Z preferred shares should be trading at $1.1875/5.25% = $22.61 which is almost exactly 10% higher. If this were to occur within the next two years, the annualized return would be a low single digit number.

Investment thesis

I’d like to emphasize I’m not interested in Wells Fargo’s preferred shares Series Z purely for the income as I’m pursuing a "hybrid" of income and capital gains. I’m aiming for a high single-digit or low double-digit total return in the next two years as lower interest rates on the financial markets should push the Series Z share price higher.

The common shares of Wells Fargo could be interesting as well, but only on market weakness. The company is currently trading at about 14 times its reported earnings for 2024, but as the consensus estimates show, the net income is expected to increase to $6.94/share in 2026, and I’d definitely be willing to pay a price in the $70s for Wells Fargo, given the robust outlook. In situations like this, I usually try to write out of the money put options while waiting for a better entry point. And in case the share price doesn’t decrease, I can just pocket the option premium. A P70 for May 2025, for instance, has an option premium of around $1. Should Wells Fargo trade below $70 at or close to expiry and should the put option get exercised, the true net cost of the position would be around $69.

My main focus is on the preferred shares, but I'm open-minded and will be keeping an eye on the common stock as well.

data.title: NIO: A Long Road To Profitability With No Guarantees
data.description:
BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) (OTCPK:BYDDF) has proven that Chinese EVs aren't just a flash in the pan. They can really be efficient and reach a global scale, rivaling traditional Western companies ranging from Ford (F) to Tesla (TSLA).

But that doesn't mean you can be optimistic about all Chinese EV makers, or hunt for a similar case, just because you think company X or Y will be the “next BYD”.

In this article, we'll delve a little deeper into NIO's (NYSE:NIO) thesis.

During the 2021 hype, the company surpassed $75B in market cap, and today trades at just under $9B, with expectations having come back down to earth.

Even with a strong correction and the company having already delivered and now not just being a company without fundamentals, NIO still faces many challenges, and to find the investment case interesting, you need a lot of conviction in the quality of the company and its cars.

A Little About The Chinese EV Market

XPeng (XPEV), Li Auto (LI) and even Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) are Chinese companies that produce electric vehicles, each with a different focus. XPeng focuses on differentiation through technology features, such as the full-stack advanced driver-assistance system (ADAS). Li Auto produces hybrid SUVs and is also a pioneer in extended-range EVs in China. BYD is now the largest EV maker, even surpassing Tesla.

BYD's case is particularly interesting, because contrary to what some might initially think, its massive sales and competitive prices are not just a reflection of Chinese subsidies and incentives, but of a series of strategic decisions and initiatives that have proven their worth over time. The company is very efficient, has vertical integration, and has managed to achieve volume through cheaper and more accessible models, allowing the company to reach a scale sufficient to achieve robust profitability figures, such as the ~$4.7B in net income over the last 12 months.

So I don't think it's something that can be extrapolated to other companies. NIO may be successful with its strategies, but it is unlikely to achieve similar feats to BYD, as this is the result of characteristics particular to the company, such as competitive pricing, verticality and a strong acceptance of the brand around the world.

NIO Has Already Reached Some Milestones

Returning to NIO, the company has been trying (and succeeding) to differentiate itself with a greater focus on services and a more premium experience, as well as battery swap, which is a practical way of making the user experience better, facilitating their migration to EVs.

The company is already gaining traction, and delivered 13.8k vehicles in Jan 2025, a very important growth (38% YoY) and with most of them being characterized as “premium smart electric vehicles”, which is its main NIO brand. The problem is that in December this figure was 31.1k, which shows that it really was a very unusual month and cannot be assumed as a recurring figure in my opinion. If the company consistently reaches ~30,000 vehicles delivered per month, it would be a very interesting milestone, but still a little far from enough to achieve a positive operating income.

Still, the number of vehicles delivered is already quite impressive and shows that the brand is managing to gain relevance in the industry. But for the sake of comparison, in Q4 Tesla maintained total deliveries at around 495k and almost 1.8 million in the year 2024.

In 2023, NIO was ranked No. 9 in China's NEV market. In the most recent chart (below), the company doesn't even appear, which reinforces that China's market is very dynamic with companies like BYD dominating, but many brands competing for this space, such as Geely (OTCPK:GELYF) and Changan (000625).

According to some reviews I've found, the quality of the cars pleases users.

Their technological features look like they could really serve as a differentiator at some point (as the company is investing heavily in R&D), as the NIO Full Stack table below shows.

According to NIO's CEO, the company will be intensively releasing new products until the end of 2025, and it seems to me that this could be a turning point for the company, since it will be optimizing its models and this could affect public perception and show whether its products will have high acceptance/demand in different regions.

In short, NIO seems to be a brand that is starting to show positive signs of positioning itself in the industry, and this is reflected in its deliveries. For instance, the company is already present in Europe, and some car review sites have shown positive views of the car.

NIO's Problems and What Needs to Happen to Overcome Them

The narrative was very strong a couple of years ago, with some comparing it to a Chinese Tesla (or next Tesla) or some describing it as a potential BYD. But even with some of the positive signs mentioned in the section above, there is still a long way to go.

The company is still far from proving its ability to produce and execute strategies on a large scale.

The lack of scale creates a strong problem for the fundamentals. The company has cash and equivalents of $3.4B, but consistently burns cash, which means that its shares outstanding often rise due to the need for cash.

This cash burn occurs even though the company has already achieved a very interesting gross margin when compared to other automotive companies. One of the main expenses is R&D, which is a positive point, as it shows that the company is making efforts to remain innovative, and may eventually reduce or dilute this expense if it manages to increase deliveries.

For NIO's fundamentals to really improve, I see a number of factors being needed, the main one being economies of scale (mass production). But for scale, the company needs to achieve other factors, such as a clearer differentiation of its product that makes buyers prefer it over another brand, and this would mitigate competition and increase deliveries. International expansion could also be a key to increasing deliveries and eventually diluting costs and expenses.

It's Very Hard To Justify NIO's Valuation

NIO is somewhere in the middle ground between a stock with fundamentals and a speculative stock.

I don't believe it's a 100% speculative case, since the company already has an interesting level of deliveries, brand recognition and a gross margin that points to a profitable future if it continues to grow.

However, its fundamentals are still far from solid, making the uncertainty even greater, and therefore making it an extremely risky stock, since it could spend years burning cash and diluting shareholders (which would be a huge destruction of value).

To try to estimate the potential and minimum that the company needs to have to stop this cash burn, I did some back of the envelope math.

If the Q3 gross profit is annualized, it comes to $1.1 billion, but expenses of $4.1B erode all that, leading to a huge loss from operations.

For a few years from now (I considered 2028), I imagined that NIO could increase its gross profit per vehicle delivered by ~15% (it would still be a gross profit of less than $6k per car, so it's a conservative assumption). If it maintains expenses (which is difficult, given that expenses usually grow in line with revenue and/or inflation), the company would still need to reach more than 700,000 vehicles delivered in a year to have a tiny operating income.

710,000 is achievable, BYD and Tesla have much higher numbers, but believing in this scenario, means believing that NIO will gain a lot of relevance in the coming years.

The good thing is that in a (very) optimistic scenario, the company could unlock a lot of value for its shareholders.

If in some year the company reaches 1,300,000 vehicles sold, a very ambitious figure, that means a price-to-operating income of ~2.5x.

Although there is this asymmetry with a lot of upsides, if the company becomes one of the biggest global EV brands, this scenario is extremely uncertain, which makes me very skeptical about its valuation and NIO's case in general.

The Bottom Line

Although NIO has already shown itself to be a company that has positive characteristics and could eventually become more relevant in the EV market, its prospects are still quite cloudy.

To have NIO in the portfolio now, you have to believe that it will be able to expand its number of deliveries by a lot, and let's remember that there are relevant headwinds in the scenario, ranging from macroeconomic factors and tariffs to intense competition.

Given these uncertainties, there is a considerable chance that the company will continue to burn cash and dilute the shareholders. That’s why my rating for NIO stock is a sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Walmart, Alibaba, Medtronic To Reports Earnings During Holiday-Shortened Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

In a holiday- and data-light week, investors will be looking at January housing data for the U.S., including housing starts on Wednesday and existing home sales on Friday. Also Friday, there will be a purchasing manager’s preliminary print by S&P Global for February and the consumer sentiment purchasing index from the University of Michigan.

Walmart (WMT), Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA), Medtronic (MDT), Occidental Petroleum (OXY), and Analog Devices (ADI) will report earnings next week.

Earnings spotlight: Monday, February 17 - UFP Industries, Noble (NE), and Transocean (RIG). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, February 18 - Arista Networks (ANET), Medtronic (MDT), Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), Occidental Petroleum, and Vulcan Materials (VMC). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, February 19 - HSBC Holdings (HSBC), Analog Devices (ADI), Carvana (CVNA), and Garmin (GRMN). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, February 20 - Walmart, Alibaba Group Holdings, Booking Holdings (BKNG), Copart (CPRT), MercadoLibre (MELI), and Southern (SO). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: MicroCloud Hologram (HOLO) and Faraday Future Intelligent Electric have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Medical Properties Trust (MPW) and Kohl's (KSS) head into the new week with a high level of short interest outstanding on them. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Firefly Neuroscience (AIFF), Anteris Technologies Global (AVR), and Triumph Group (TGI). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Venture Global (VG), Columbus McKinnon (CMCO), and Brazil Potash (GRO).

IPO watch: Anbio Biotechnology (NNNN) is expected to price its IPO on Tuesday and start trading on Wednesday. Apimeds Pharmaceuticals is scheduled to price its IPO on Thursday and begin trading the following day. The lockup periods for Reitar Logtech (RITR) and WORK Medical Technology Group (WOK) expire Wednesday.

data.title: My Dividend Horse BDC Yields 8%: Ares Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

It's often said that price is what you pay, but value is what you get.

When I was younger, I did not understand what this meant. I felt that the price that I paid determined the value of the item. As I've gotten older, I understand this concept more and more deeply. Whenever I'm getting ready to go out the door, I have a specific jacket that I typically grab. I've had it for over seven years. I throw it on and it fits perfectly. It has been with me through thick and thin. I can't even remember the price I paid for it anymore, but I know the value it's provided me over these last seven years. I have a pair of boots that I will always put on; they have my footprint formed into the insole and fit me like a glove. It would be uncomfortable for anyone else to wear them, given how much it has formed around my foot. I can't remember the price I paid for those either, but I can tell you the value and the quality of those products.

When it comes to building a portfolio that will carry you for decades throughout your retirement, you don't necessarily want to worry off-hand about the price that you're paying. You definitely don't want to pay too much. But when it comes to a decade later, are you going to truly remember the exact price you paid for the shares, especially if the company has continued to provide you value over those decades? This is one reason why it always makes me chuckle when I see commenters on my articles a month later decrying how bad a choice it was because a month has passed, and they felt that the short-term price has dropped too much. Their perspective needs to be shifted. When I buy something, I am buying a value that will pay out to me over decades, not 30 days.

Today, I want to look at one of my oldest holdings in my portfolio and discuss how it is performing while recognizing the true value it has provided over time.

Let's dive in!

My Old Reliable BDC

Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC), yielding 8.3%, is a BDC (Business Development Company) that provides lending and management assistance to middle-market companies within the United States. BDCs play an important role in providing liquidity to the massive section of the economy. Middle-market firms generated over one-third of the private sector GDP (Gross Domestic Product) in the United States.

ARCC is among the oldest investments in the High Dividend Opportunities Model Portfolio. ARCC has paid us dividends through trade wars, COVID-19, interest rate cuts, and interest rate hikes. We've covered it numerous times, but with the recent earnings release, it was time for an update.

ARCC is the largest publicly traded BDC and, at 20 years old, is a BDC that has been truly tested through the whole economic cycle. The BDC sector is relatively young and has really boomed since the Great Financial Crisis. As a result, most BDCs haven't been through a real recession.

In 2024, ARCC continued its expansion. Making $15 billion in new investments and growing its portfolio to $26.7 billion. Up from $22.9 billion in 2023. Source.

From this portfolio, ARCC saw Core EPS of $0.55/share for Q4 and $2.33 for the year. Note that this is lower than it saw in 2023, and that is due to interest rates being lower. BDCs borrow at fixed-rates and lend at floating rates, a business model that saw many temporary benefits as interest rates went up.

ARCC saw its NAV (Net Asset Value) increase to $19.89. A record high, up from $19.24 at the end of 2023.

While many peers opted to pay out supplemental dividends, ARCC decided to retain capital and maintain a regular dividend of $0.48/quarter. This helped NAV grow, and puts ARCC in a very strong position to grow its portfolio while maintaining a low leverage ratio.

Over the year, ARCC made its portfolio more conservative, with a focus on first-lien investments. First lien holdings went from 44% in 2023 to 57% in 2024:

ARCC did see a bump up in non-accruals in Q4. They rose to 1.7% of the portfolio on a cost basis, which is similar to Q1 2024.

This is a normal variation for ARCC, which has seen numerous periods where non-accruals were higher. For example, here is the same slide from Q4 2023: Source.

ARCC is currently trading at about a ~15% premium to NAV. That is at the high end of ARCC's trading range.

ARCC is an anchor that is a great addition to any portfolio. You can expect ARCC to continue to provide ample income and remain a leader in the BDC space.

Conclusion

Buying and holding ARCC over a long period of time will massively benefit your portfolio. They are the oldest BDC for a reason because they've outlived peers and outperformed new companies that have arisen since. They're the type of holding that you can put in your portfolio, and they will, like a good pair of boots formed to your foot, provide you a comfortable experience through your retirement by raining down income from the stock market into your portfolio.

ARCC is helmed by an exceptional management team that can unlock value while providing massively needed liquidity to the middle market. The private companies that you have no other way of impacting, helping, or owning unless you buy them outright privately. The BDC sector is filled with plenty of interesting names, ideas, and options, but today, we've looked at the oldest and the largest.

My goal for every single reader of my articles, whether they ascribe to High Dividend Opportunities' Income Method or whether they have their own methodology, is to have the very best retirement possible. Your retirement's success does not mean my retirement's failure. This idea that we need to actively compete against one another and beat each other down because we're outperforming one another is a folly that too many of us fall into. Instead, you can succeed, and I can succeed independently of one another without harming each other. This is one reason why thousands of people see successful retirements while being income investors and dividend investors without harming the thousands of others who choose their method. ARCC provides wonderful income and wonderful total returns. It's a kind of holding that every type of investor can benefit from.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: YETI: Losing Patience Amid Slowing Sales (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To me, the markets in 2025 require active portfolio management. We must consistently monitor the fundamental performance of each of our positions, especially during earnings season, to trim the stocks that are no longer performing up to scruff. After all, equity valuations are tremendously high, while short-term bonds still yield a very attractive risk-free ~5%.

YETI (NYSE:YETI) is a stock that I'm keen to remove from my portfolio after its recent Q4 earnings print. The maker of heavy-duty coolers has suffered through slowing sales, as well as potential brand damage from product recalls that happened in 2023. YETI's stock, down ~20% over the past year, remains trading at an appealing valuation: but at the same time, the company is range-bound as it fails to re-invigorate its growth rates.

I last wrote a bullish note on YETI in early January, when the company was trading at similar ~$38 levels. Since then, the company has suffered through a material deceleration in revenue growth rates, while also failing to advance its margin profile. To me, the stock's cheap valuation no longer is worth waiting for this brand to turn its sales around. As such, I am downgrading my viewpoint on YETI to neutral.

The biggest risk that I see to YETI is a fading brand. The company's core products, coolers and drinkware, are heavy-duty products that last a long time. With slow replacement cycles, perhaps the majority of consumers who want a YETI product already have a good one. In addition, the company's appeal to new buyers may be hampered by its product recalls.

Now, these recalls are not "new news" for YETI. The company discovered that a number of its coolers emitted unhealthy magnets that could accidentally be ingested, prompting YETI to enact a voluntary recall of its product. The issue, as revealed in YETI's Q4 results, was that far more consumers accepted the recall offer than the company anticipated. The company adjusted its reserves for recalls in Q4, taking a hit to revenue - but perhaps more importantly, the wider base of consumers sending their products back is a bigger blow to YETI's reputation.

To me, this risk now counterweights the remaining positives I see on the company, which are as follows:

  • Strong international growth. While YETI's sales growth in the U.S. has drifted close to zero, it continues to see near-30% growth overseas.
  • Huge gross margin profile. As a maker of heavy-duty products, the company's low-60s gross margins showcases the premium nature of its brand and its ability to charge high prices.

To me, however, the risk of slowing sales counteracts YETI's fundamental positives as well as its cheap valuation, and I'm no longer willing to hold onto this stock in my portfolio as it continues to drag. Keep watching YETI closely, but start trimming your position here.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through YETI's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

YETI's revenue grew only 5% y/y to $546.5 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $551.9 million (+6% y/y). The company's update to its recall reserves (which was driven by a greater trend of customers turning in their items) impacted revenue growth by two points: but even when we adjust for this one-time revision, YETI's adjusted revenue growth of 7% y/y still decelerated three points relative to 10% y/y growth in Q3.

We note that this slowdown in growth is a telling signal of the brand's slowdown, especially during the winter holiday season. We note as well that sales to wholesale partners increased only 2% y/y.

Retailers de-stocking their shelves amid a tougher macro climate in which consumers have pulled back on spending is nothing new: many consumer products companies have reported similar trends. And yet YETI, which only has a small retail store fleet in addition to its e-commerce site, needs wholesalers to keep prominently placing YETI products in their stores. The near-zero growth in wholesale revenue is another red flag that could further diminish YETI's brand visibility.

The company notes as well that the promotional environment has been very heavy in the fourth quarter, per CEO Matt Reintjes' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

While we observed increased competition and promotional intensity in the market, YETI continued past practice of using targeted promotions in these high noise periods. Additionally, following the launch of our YETI ID program last quarter, we observed a significant increase in account creation enabling deeper engagement with our customers and a more unique and personalized experience with the brand. Amazon continues to perform very well as our team drives strong operational efficiencies across the marketplace, effectively reaching consumers who shop that channel. In corporate sales, we saw robust growth across all regions with strong inbound sales performance domestically and great traction in international markets through our ongoing execution."

On the profitability front, we note that YETI maintained its operating margin profile at 19.9%, adjusted for the product recalls, roughly flat versus 19.8% in the year-ago quarter.

We still appreciate the company's ability to maintain its ~60% gross margins as well as maintain opex discipline: at the same time, however, its overall earnings growth is too slow to justify investing in this name.

Valuation and key takeaways

For the current fiscal year FY25, YETI expects revenue to keep slogging along at a 5-7% y/y pace, while also implying barely any margin expansion with just 5.5-7.5% expected growth in operating income.

Meanwhile, the company is guiding to $2.90-$2.95 in pro forma EPS, or ~7% growth at the midpoint - with the spread of EPS growth over revenue growth contributed by share buybacks, with $450 million remaining under its current authorization ($350 million was freshly added in Q4; the current total authorization represents a hefty ~14% of the company's current market cap). At current share prices near $38, YETI trades at a 13.0x FY25 P/E ratio.

YETI is certainly a value stock in an expensive market, but this cheap valuation also reflects the company's slow-growth reality and a slew of issues ranging from slow wholesale expansion to damaging product recalls. In my view, it's best to move to the sidelines here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Q2: Reaching $40 Billion In Revenue In FY26
data.description:
I reiterated a ‘Buy’ rating on Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) in December 2024, highlighting its undervalued stock price. Super Micro Computer delivered 54% revenue growth in Q2 FY25, and anticipates $40 billion in revenue in FY26. I am encouraged by the company’s recent convertible notes issuance and the ramp-up of full production for Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell Rack-Scale Solutions. I reiterate a 'Buy' rating with a one-year price target of $70 per share.

Ramp-Up of Full Production for Blackwell Rack-Scale Solutions

Super Micro Computer released its Q2 FY25 preliminary result on February 11th after the market close, reporting 54.2% revenue growth at the midpoint. As shown in the chart below, Super Micro Computer’s revenue growth has decelerated in recent quarters, due to the shortages of Blackwell chips and recent internal turmoil.

As noted during the earnings call, Super Micro Computer’s utilization rate is only 55% in the US, 60% in Taiwan and 1% in Malaysia. As such, I think the company has already built sufficient capacities to capture the rapid growth of AI and data center demands.

On February 5th, the company announced the ramp-up of full production for Nvidia’s Blackwell Rack-Scale Solutions with NVIDIA HGX B200. Nvidia has already certified Super Micro Computer’s systems with H100 and H200 GPUs. I think the ramp up of full production is a huge milestone for Super Micro Computer and the new liquid-cooled 4U NVIDIA HGX B200 8-GPU system could potentially capture tremendous business growth in the fast-growing data center market.

In addition, Super Micro Computer announced private placements of $700 million in 2.25% convertible senior notes due 2028 on February 11. The convertible notes issuance could provide additional capital for the company to invest in the latest liquid-cooling technology, Datacenter Building Block Solutions, as well as new GPU architectures. I think the debt issuance mitigated the capital deficiency risk for the company, at least for the next three years.

Lastly, the company has been working with the new auditor and targets filing FY24 10K, Q1 FY25 and Q2 FY25 by February 25th, 2025. As discussed in my previous articles, their Special Committee hasn’t found any kind of accounting fraud or inappropriate revenue recognition.

Outlook and Valuation

For FY25, the company reduced its revenue guidance from the previous $26 billion - $30 billion to $23.5 billion-$25 billion. I think the guidance cut is quite reasonable considering the delays in Blackwell chip production as well as their 10K filing delays. I think some large enterprise customers might hold off on their orders with Super Micro Computer until the regulatory filing is completed, which could affect Super Micro Computer’s growth in FY25.

The management continues to guide toward a $40 billion revenue target for FY26, and expressed strong confidence about the near-term growth during the earnings call.

For the near-term growth, I am considering the following factors:

  • I anticipate the company will face near-term growth headwinds from its regulatory issues. Over the earnings call, the management indicated that the strong backlog from data center could provide them great visibilities for the growth in FY25 and FY26. I forecast 65% annual growth in FY25 and FY26, aligned with the management’s expectations.
  • From FY27 onwards, I anticipate the AI market will shift from AI training stage to inference. As such, the AI server customers will gradually shift from major hyperscalers to enterprise customers. I forecast the company will grow its revenue by 30%, aligned with the AI market growth. Due to the scale, I assume the revenue growth rate will slow to 20% from FY29 onwards, followed by 10% growth from FY32 onwards.
  • I acknowledge that AI server is highly competitive. There were many questions regarding the gross margin trend during the earnings call. I only assume 10bps margin expansion driven by operating leverage, driven by 10bps gross margin headwinds and 20bps margin expansion driven by reduction in SG&A expenses.

I updated the cost of equity to 14.4% assuming: risk free rate 4.6%; beta 1.8; equity risk premium 6%. The recent debt issuance has been incorporated in the model. I calculate the free cash flow from equity (FCFE) as follows:

Please note that there is a big gap between its FCF and FCFE, as I anticipate Super Micro Computer will require additional working capital to support its fast growth in AI server, particularly for inventory and account receivables, as detailed in the table below. The working capital requirements will reduce the free cash flow generation, but won't affect the amount of FCFE as the change in working capital will be added back.

Discounting all the future FCFE to the end of FY25, the one-year target price is calculated to be $70 per share, as per my estimates.

Key Risk

I think the biggest risk is the regulatory filing. While the management is confident about their audit progress, it remains uncertain whether the company can file its 10K before the deadline.

Conclusion

While Super Micro Computer’s growth has been affected by the Blackwell chip shortages and regulatory filing delays, the long-term growth trajectory in AI servers remains intact, and the company is well positioned in the liquid-cooling server market. I reiterate a 'Buy' rating with a one-year price target of $70 per share.

data.title: Equinor: Undervalued With Market-Beating Potential
data.description:
Building a diversified portfolio generally means gaining exposure to different industries. However, that could also mean getting exposure to international equities, some of which can trade at compelling discounts to their U.S. counterparts.

European stocks have become favorable again as the STOXX Europe 600 Index (OTC:DJXXF) has rallied by 7% so far this year in U.S. dollar terms, while the FTSE 100 (VWO) has risen by 6%, comparing favorably the 4% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY).

This brings me to Equinor (NYSE:EQNR), which apparently has not seen the same level of enthusiasm, as it’s declined by 1% since the start of this year. As shown below, EQNR hasn’t done much of anything over the past year, with an 8.6% decline over the past 12 months, all while paying out hefty dividends, both regular and special.

In this article, I visit EQNR including recent business results and outlook, and discuss why income and value investors may want to consider layering into EQNR at its current discounted valuation, so let’s dive in!

Why EQNR?

Equinor is a leading Norwegian energy company with diversified assets that span across oil, gas, renewables, and low-carbon solutions. It operates in the Norwegian Continental Shelf and internationally, including the Gulf of Mexico, Africa and Asia.

What makes EQNR attractive is its expertise and exposure to deepwater drilling. This form of exploration and production is more profitable than other extraction methods due to comparatively larger reserves and a long production life span. While the initial cost is higher to that of onshore shale, the longer production span means a greater cashflow stability.

According to Wood Mackenzie, offshore drilling is expected to increase materially over the coming years with production from deepwater expected to surge by 60% by the end of this decade. Deepwater drilling has been a focus for Big Oil as shallower waters mature and production declines, while demand for oil has been rising. That means deepwater players like Petrobras (PBR) and Equinor in this case are in solid position to benefit.

Meanwhile, EQNR saw strong performance in 2024, achieving a 21% ROCE (return on capital employed). This compares favorably to even Exxon Mobil (XOM), which itself is an industry leader with a high ROCE of 17%, as XOM’s management noted in its recent 2024 earnings call. EQNR’s oil and gas production grew by 4% during 2024, driven by strong performance from the Johan Sverdrup and Troll fields on the Norwegian Continental Shelf. Plus, while renewable energy carries a stigma of being lower return, EQNR was able to achieve a 10% return on equity on these assets.

Looking ahead, management raised its guidance to more than 10% oil and gas production growth from 2024 levels to 2027. This means that by 2030, production is expected to reach 2.2 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, which is up from 2 million boe/day in the previous outlook. At the same time, EQNR plans to reduce its renewals capital spend by 50% compared to previous guidance, opting instead to focus on high return oil and gas production. Management’s outlook is driven by strong demand for oil and gas, as noted during the recent earnings call:

Energy demand is growing. We expect higher production growth. Market and political uncertainty is high. We are robust and set up to create value from volatility. Global oil demand grows and expected above 100 million barrels through this decade. For gas, we expect demand to increase and stay above today's level all the way to 2050. Asia drives demand short-term and we see U.S. increasing going forward.

Importantly, EQNR carries a strong balance sheet and an AA- credit rating with stable outlook from S&P. This is supported by $23.5 billion in cash and short-term investments, and by a low debt to capital of 12%, sitting below even that of industry giant ExxonMobil’s 13% ratio.

EQNR expects to generate $23 billion in free cash flow annually between now and 2027, driven by production growth and an $8 billion reduction in capital spend. This FCF more than covers the $9 billion that management expects to pay out through dividends this year and a $5 billion share buyback this year on top of that, equating to 7.7% of EQNR’s current market cap. Also encouraging, EQNR recently raised the regular dividend rate by 5.7% to $0.37 and expects to pay extraordinary dividends this year based on 45% of cash flow from operations.

Lastly, I find EQNR to be attractive at the current price of $23.41 with a forward PE of 6.9, sitting well below its normal PE of 10.7, as shown below. At the current valuation, EQNR trades at less than half the valuation of its American peers ExxonMobil and Chevron (CVX), which carry PE ratios 14.9 and 15.0, respectively.

With a 6.3% regular yield plus potential for extraordinary dividends, my expectations for EPS growth in the mid-single digit as a conservative base case and potential for a reversion to mean valuation, EQNR could well deliver potential market-beating total returns from here.

Risks to the thesis include potential for oil and gas price volatility, which could pressure profitability. Moreover, changes in global demand due to macroeconomic factors could also impact results. Plus, any potential accidents or spills could result in liabilities for EQNR and challenges in the renewable energy market could be a headwind for returns on investment.

Investor Takeaway

Equinor presents a compelling opportunity for income and value investors due to its discounted valuation, robust financial health, and promising growth outlook. It demonstrated strong performance in 2024, highlighted by a 21% ROCE and 4% production growth. Plus, EQNR is focused on high-return deepwater drilling, reduced renewables capital spend, and commitment to shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks. With a forward PE significantly below its historical average and U.S. counterparts, EQNR offers high income and potential for market-beating total returns from its current discounted valuation.
